heroOverview: Kidney stones are hard mineral deposits that form inside the kidneys when urine becomes concentrated enough for salts — most often calcium oxalate — to crystallise and clump together. They affect roughly 1 in 11 people, and lifetime risk in the United States has climbed to about 11% in men and 9% in women. The hallmark is renal colic: sudden, severe, wavelike pain in the flank that can radiate to the groin as a stone moves into the ureter. Most small stones pass on their own with fluids and pain control, but larger stones can block urine flow, cause infection, and damage the kidney. Recurrence is common, which makes prevention as important as treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime risk ~11% in men and ~9% in women; about 1 in 11 people affected (NIDDK, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Peak onset ages 30-60; historically more common in men, though the sex gap is narrowing
keyStats.avgAge: Most first stones occur between ages 30 and 50
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence has roughly doubled over the past few decades in industrialised countries

definition: A kidney stone (ICD-10: N20.0), medically termed nephrolithiasis, is a solid concretion formed from minerals and salts that crystallise out of urine within the kidney's collecting system. Stones form when the urine becomes supersaturated with stone-forming substances, when natural crystallisation inhibitors such as citrate are deficient, or both. Calcium-based stones account for about 80% of cases, split between calcium oxalate and, less often, calcium phosphate. The remainder are uric acid stones (around 9%), which form in persistently acidic urine; struvite or infection stones (around 10%), which grow rapidly in the presence of urea-splitting bacteria; and rare cystine stones caused by an inherited transport defect. The condition falls under urology, with nephrology involvement when stones reflect a metabolic or kidney disorder. Stones become symptomatic when they obstruct urine flow, classically as they pass from the kidney into the narrow ureter. Incidence has risen alongside obesity, diabetes, and warmer climates, all of which raise urinary stone risk.

typesClassification.description: The most common type, about 60-70% of all stones, formed from calcium combining with oxalate; linked to low urine volume, high oxalate intake, and low urinary citrate.
Around 10-15% of stones, more common in alkaline urine and conditions such as renal tubular acidosis or hyperparathyroidism.
About 9% of stones, formed in persistently acidic urine; associated with gout, obesity, diabetes, and high purine intake, and notably they do not show up on plain X-rays.
Roughly 10% of stones, made of magnesium ammonium phosphate; they form only when urea-splitting bacteria infect the urinary tract and can grow large enough to fill the kidney as staghorn stones.
A rare type, around 1%, caused by the inherited disorder cystinuria, which leads to recurrent stones from childhood.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, severe, cramping pain in the flank or side that comes in waves (renal colic) as the stone moves and the ureter spasms around it.
Pain that radiates from the back or flank down toward the lower abdomen and groin as the stone descends the ureter.
Visible pink, red, or brown blood in the urine, or microscopic blood detected on testing, from the stone abrading the urinary tract lining.
Pain or burning during urination, particularly once the stone reaches the lower ureter near the bladder.
A persistent, urgent need to urinate and passing only small amounts, common when the stone is close to the bladder.
Nausea and vomiting, triggered by shared nerve pathways between the kidney and the gastrointestinal tract during a colic episode.
Cloudy or foul-smelling urine, which can signal an accompanying infection.
Restlessness and inability to find a comfortable position — unlike abdominal conditions where people lie still, stone pain makes people pace.
Fever and chills, which are not part of an uncomplicated stone and signal infection behind an obstruction — a medical emergency.
Difficulty passing urine or a sudden drop in urine output if a stone blocks the only functioning kidney or both ureters.
earlyWarningSigns: A vague, intermittent ache in one side of the back before frank colic begins.
Urine that looks slightly pink or tea-coloured.
Mild discomfort with urination without an obvious infection.
A history of previous stones, since recurrence often announces itself the same way.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38°C with flank pain, which suggests an infected, obstructed kidney needing urgent drainage.
Uncontrollable pain despite oral pain medication, or pain with persistent vomiting.
A sharp drop in urine output or no urine at all, especially in someone with a single kidney.
Signs of sepsis — confusion, a racing heart, dizziness, or feeling profoundly unwell.

causes.description: Inadequate fluid intake, heavy sweating, or living in a hot climate concentrates the urine, pushing stone-forming salts past their solubility limit so they crystallise; it is the single most common and most correctable cause.
Oxalate-rich foods raise urinary oxalate, while a high-salt diet increases urinary calcium excretion; together they make calcium oxalate stone formation considerably more likely.

Citrate normally binds calcium in the urine and inhibits crystal growth; conditions and diets that lower citrate, including chronic diarrhoea and high animal-protein intake, remove this natural protection.

Primary hyperparathyroidism raises blood and urine calcium, gout and high purine turnover produce uric acid stones, and renal tubular acidosis favours calcium phosphate stones; each drives recurrent stone disease until the underlying condition is treated.

causes.cause: Urinary tract infection with urea-splitting bacteria
causes.description: Bacteria such as Proteus break down urea, alkalinise the urine, and provide the chemical conditions for struvite stones, which can grow rapidly into large staghorn calculi.

An autosomal recessive defect in the kidney's reabsorption of cystine leaves high concentrations of this poorly soluble amino acid in the urine, producing recurrent stones that often begin in childhood.

riskFactors.description: The dominant modifiable risk; people who keep urine output above about 2-2.5 litres a day roughly halve their recurrence rate compared with low-volume drinkers.
Higher body mass index raises urinary calcium, oxalate, and uric acid and lowers urine pH; obesity is associated with roughly a 30-40% higher stone risk.

riskFactors.factor: Diet high in salt, animal protein, and oxalate
riskFactors.description: Excess sodium increases urinary calcium, animal protein lowers citrate and urine pH, and concentrated oxalate intake feeds calcium oxalate stones.
A single stone predicts recurrence; without preventive measures, roughly 50% of people form another stone within 5-10 years.
Having a first-degree relative with stones roughly doubles personal risk, reflecting shared genetics in calcium handling and inherited disorders such as cystinuria.

riskFactors.factor: Hot, dry climate and certain occupations
riskFactors.description: Heat and outdoor or industrial work increase sweat losses and urine concentration; stone incidence is measurably higher in hot regions.

riskFactors.factor: Medical conditions — hyperparathyroidism, gout, diabetes, IBD
riskFactors.description: Each alters urinary chemistry: hyperparathyroidism raises calcium, gout and diabetes lower urine pH, and inflammatory bowel disease causes fat malabsorption that drives up oxalate absorption.
High-dose vitamin C, calcium supplements taken away from food, topiramate, and some diuretics can raise stone risk; the contributions are reviewed when stones recur.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-60, the peak window for a first stone
Men slightly more than women, though the gap is closing
People living in hot, arid regions or working in heat
People with obesity, type 2 diabetes, or gout
Those with a family history of stones or inherited cystinuria
People with inflammatory bowel disease or prior bariatric surgery
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with the clinical picture: sudden severe one-sided flank pain that radiates to the groin, with blood in the urine and a restless patient, strongly suggests a stone. The history covers prior stones, fluid habits, diet, family history, and any infection symptoms. Urinalysis confirms blood and screens for infection and crystals, while blood tests check kidney function and calcium. Imaging confirms the diagnosis and guides management: a non-contrast CT scan of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder is the gold standard, detecting more than 95% of stones and showing their size and location precisely. Ultrasound is preferred first in pregnant women and is a reasonable, radiation-free initial test in others, though it can miss small or mid-ureteric stones. A plain abdominal X-ray shows most calcium stones but misses uric acid stones, which are radiolucent. When a stone is passed or retrieved, it is sent for composition analysis, because the stone type determines prevention. In recurrent or complex stone-formers, a metabolic workup adds a 24-hour urine collection to measure volume, calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, and pH. The main differentials to exclude are pyelonephritis, appendicitis, ovarian and ectopic pregnancy problems, diverticulitis, and a leaking aortic aneurysm in older patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard test, pinpointing stone size, location, and density to guide treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still on a table that slides through the scanner for a few minutes; no contrast injection is needed and the scan itself is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A radiation-free way to detect stones and, importantly, hydronephrosis (urine backing up); the preferred first test in pregnancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A technician moves a gel-covered probe over your back and abdomen for 15-20 minutes; it is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects blood, infection, and crystals, and identifies bacteria if an infected stone is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a urine sample in a cup; basic results are immediate, culture takes 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (kidney function, calcium, uric acid)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses how well the kidneys are working and screens for metabolic causes such as hyperparathyroidism or gout.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm, with results usually back within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the exact stone type, which dictates the prevention strategy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: If you pass a stone, you strain your urine to catch it and send it to the lab; no procedure is needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures urine volume and the balance of calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, and pH to tailor prevention in recurrent stone-formers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect all urine over a full day into a provided container kept cool, then return it to the lab.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on stone size, location, symptoms, and whether there is infection or obstruction. For an uncomplicated stone smaller than about 5 mm, the great majority pass on their own; management is supportive with strong pain relief, usually a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory such as diclofenac or ketorolac, anti-nausea medication, and generous fluids. For stones between roughly 5 and 10 mm in the ureter, medical expulsive therapy with an alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin can relax the ureter and improve passage rates, and a brief trial of passage is reasonable if the patient is comfortable and not infected. Stones that are too large to pass, that fail a trial of passage, or that cause uncontrolled pain need active removal. Shock wave lithotripsy uses focused energy to fragment stones from outside the body and suits smaller kidney and upper-ureteric stones. Ureteroscopy passes a thin scope up the urinary tract to break the stone with a laser and is highly effective for ureteric stones, with stone-free rates above 90%. Percutaneous nephrolithotomy, in which a stone is removed through a small flank incision, is reserved for large or staghorn stones. An infected, obstructed kidney is a urological emergency: it needs urgent drainage with a ureteric stent or nephrostomy tube plus antibiotics, with definitive stone treatment delayed until the infection is controlled. The American Urological Association and European Association of Urology guidelines underpin these choices. Whatever the acute treatment, preventing the next stone — through fluids, diet, and treating any metabolic cause — is an essential second half of care.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAID analgesia (diclofenac, ketorolac)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pain control for renal colic; NSAIDs reduce ureteric spasm and inflammation and outperform opioids for this specific pain in trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide effective pain relief in roughly 75-90% of colic episodes and reduce the need for rescue opioids.

medicalTreatments.name: Medical expulsive therapy (tamsulosin)
medicalTreatments.description: An alpha-blocker that relaxes the smooth muscle of the lower ureter, easing the passage of stones in the 5-10 mm range over a two to four week trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases spontaneous passage of larger ureteric stones by roughly 10-25 percentage points compared with no therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Potassium citrate
medicalTreatments.description: Raises urinary citrate and pH; used to prevent calcium oxalate and uric acid stones and, at higher pH targets, can help dissolve uric acid stones.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stone recurrence by approximately 35-50% in citrate-responsive recurrent stone-formers.

medicalTreatments.name: Thiazide diuretics
medicalTreatments.description: Lower urinary calcium excretion, making them a mainstay of prevention in people with calcium stones and high urine calcium.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cut new stone formation by roughly 50% in randomised trials of recurrent calcium stone-formers.

medicalTreatments.name: Allopurinol
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces uric acid production; used to prevent uric acid stones and calcium oxalate stones driven by high urinary uric acid.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lowers recurrence of uric acid and hyperuricosuric calcium stones by around 30-50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for infected stones
medicalTreatments.description: Culture-directed antibiotics are required whenever a stone is associated with infection, alongside drainage and eventual stone clearance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential to prevent progression to sepsis; cure of the infection depends on also removing the stone.

surgicalOptions.name: Ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy
surgicalOptions.description: A thin scope is passed up through the bladder and ureter to locate the stone, which is fragmented with a holmium laser and the pieces removed or left to pass; often a temporary stent is placed afterward.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates above 90% for ureteric stones, the highest of the minimally invasive options.

surgicalOptions.name: Shock wave lithotripsy (SWL)
surgicalOptions.description: Focused acoustic shock waves delivered from outside the body break the stone into small fragments that then pass in the urine; entirely non-invasive but less effective for large or hard stones.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of roughly 50-80% depending on stone size, location, and composition.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
surgicalOptions.description: For large kidney stones over about 2 cm and staghorn calculi, a tract is made through the flank into the kidney to fragment and remove the stone directly.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of approximately 85-95% for large and complex stones in a single procedure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased fluid intake
alternativeTreatments.description: Not alternative so much as foundational — raising daily urine output above 2-2.5 litres is proven to roughly halve recurrence and is the cornerstone of every prevention plan.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary citrate (lemon and citrus)
alternativeTreatments.description: Lemon juice and other citrus raise urinary citrate modestly and can be a useful adjunct to medical prevention, though they do not replace potassium citrate where that is indicated.

whySeeSpecialist: Many first, small stones are managed by emergency or primary care, but a urologist should be involved when a stone is too large to pass, when pain or vomiting cannot be controlled, when there is any sign of infection behind an obstruction, or when stones recur. A urologist provides the procedural options — ureteroscopy, lithotripsy, and PCNL — and works with nephrology on metabolic prevention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a urologist with a specific stone-disease or endourology focus and access to CT imaging, a holmium laser, lithotripsy, and a metabolic stone clinic for prevention. For recurrent stone-formers, ask whether the practice routinely performs stone composition analysis and 24-hour urine studies, since prevention is only as good as the workup behind it.
preventionStrategies: Drink enough fluid, mostly water, to keep daily urine output above 2-2.5 litres — the single most effective preventive step.
Reduce dietary salt, which lowers the urinary calcium that feeds calcium stones.
Keep dietary calcium normal rather than low; adequate calcium with meals binds oxalate in the gut and lowers stone risk.
Moderate animal protein and very high-oxalate foods, and add citrus for natural citrate.
Treat underlying conditions — hyperparathyroidism, gout, recurrent urinary infection — that drive stone formation.
Use targeted medication such as thiazides, potassium citrate, or allopurinol when a 24-hour urine study identifies a specific abnormality.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a water bottle and drink steadily through the day, more in hot weather or during exercise.
Aim for pale, straw-coloured urine as a simple hydration gauge.
Season food lightly and check labels for hidden sodium in processed foods.
Keep a normal-calcium diet from food sources rather than cutting calcium out.
Limit sugary drinks, especially those sweetened with fructose, which raise stone risk.
Maintain a healthy weight, since obesity measurably increases stone formation.
If you have passed a stone, strain your urine to catch the next one for analysis.

recommended: Plenty of water spread through the day to keep urine dilute
Normal dietary calcium from milk, yoghurt, and cheese taken with meals
Citrus fruits and lemon water for their natural citrate
Fruits and vegetables generally, which raise urinary citrate and pH
avoid: Excess salt and heavily processed, high-sodium foods
Large amounts of animal protein at every meal
Concentrated high-oxalate foods in excess, such as spinach, rhubarb, and nuts, particularly for calcium oxalate stone-formers

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise supports a healthy weight and lowers stone risk, and there is no need to avoid activity because of a stone history. The main caution is hydration: replace fluid lost in sweat during and after exercise, especially in hot conditions, so that urine does not become concentrated.
dailyManagement: Track daily fluid intake until a steady habit of pale urine is established.
Take preventive medication such as potassium citrate or a thiazide exactly as prescribed.
Strain urine during an active stone episode and keep any stone passed for analysis.
Keep follow-up appointments for repeat imaging or 24-hour urine studies.
Seek urgent care if pain escalates, fever develops, or urine output drops.
prognosis: The outlook for an individual stone is generally good: about 80% of stones smaller than 4 mm and a smaller proportion of those 5-7 mm pass spontaneously, usually within one to four weeks, and modern procedures clear nearly all stones that need intervention. The dominant long-term issue is recurrence — without preventive measures, roughly half of stone-formers develop another stone within 5-10 years, but a structured prevention plan based on stone composition and 24-hour urine results can cut that risk substantially, often by half or more. Complications are uncommon when stones are managed promptly; the serious exceptions are an infected obstructed kidney, which can progress to sepsis, and repeated or prolonged obstruction, which can permanently reduce kidney function. Staghorn stones, if left untreated, carry a meaningful long-term risk of kidney loss and were historically associated with significant mortality, which is why they are removed.
recoveryTimeline: A stone that is going to pass on its own usually does so within one to four weeks, with the worst colic lasting hours at a time as the stone moves. After ureteroscopy, most people are back to normal activity within a few days, though a temporary stent can cause bladder discomfort until it is removed at one to two weeks. Shock wave lithotripsy is an outpatient procedure with recovery over a few days as fragments pass. Percutaneous surgery for large stones involves a hospital stay of one to three days and a recovery of one to two weeks.
complications: Obstruction and hydronephrosis — a stone blocks urine flow and the kidney swells; persistent or bilateral obstruction can damage kidney function and needs prompt drainage.
Infected obstructed kidney (pyonephrosis) — infection trapped behind a stone; signalled by fever and rigors with flank pain, it is an emergency requiring urgent drainage and antibiotics.
Urosepsis — bacteria from an infected obstructed system enter the bloodstream; watch for confusion, a racing pulse, and feeling profoundly unwell.
Recurrent stones — the most common long-term complication; recurrence is the rule rather than the exception without prevention.
Chronic kidney disease — repeated or prolonged obstruction, and some metabolic stone disorders, can gradually reduce kidney function over years.
Ureteric stricture — scarring of the ureter after a long-impacted stone or its treatment, which can cause ongoing partial obstruction.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Kidney Stones
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of kidney stone types, symptoms, treatment, and prevention from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Kidney Stones
supportResources.description: Education on stone types, risk factors, and prevention strategies from the National Kidney Foundation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Kidney Stones
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on recognising, treating, and preventing kidney stones, including when to seek urgent care.

sources.title: Pearle MS et al. Medical Management of Kidney Stones: AUA Guideline (2014, amended)
Assimos D et al. Surgical Management of Stones: AUA/Endourological Society Guideline (2016)
Scales CD et al. Prevalence of Kidney Stones in the United States. European Urology, 2012
Fink HA et al. Medical Management to Prevent Recurrent Nephrolithiasis in Adults: Systematic Review. Annals of Internal Medicine, 2013
NIDDK — Kidney Stones in Adults: Diagnosis and Treatment
NICE NG118 — Renal and ureteric stones: assessment and management
EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis (European Association of Urology)

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A UTI causes burning urination, frequency, and often fever with infected urine, while a stone causes severe wavelike flank pain radiating to the groin — though the two can occur together when a stone obstructs an infected system.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute kidney injury is a rapid loss of kidney function shown by rising blood creatinine, sometimes caused by an obstructing stone but also by many non-stone causes; it is a functional diagnosis rather than the stone itself.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout causes sudden severe joint pain, classically in the big toe, from uric acid crystals in joints; it shares a metabolic link with uric acid kidney stones but the pain is articular, not flank pain.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Repeated or prolonged stone obstruction, and some metabolic stone disorders, can gradually reduce kidney function.

relatedConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Infection both causes struvite stones and complicates obstructing stones, making the two closely linked.

relatedConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: A key metabolic cause of recurrent calcium stones, identified during the workup of stone-formers.

relatedConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Bladder outlet obstruction from an enlarged prostate promotes urinary stasis and bladder stone formation in older men.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have a kidney stone?
faqs.answer: The classic sign of a kidney stone is sudden, severe, cramping pain that comes in waves in one side of your back or flank and radiates toward the lower abdomen and groin. It is often accompanied by blood in the urine, nausea, and an inability to get comfortable. Pain this severe, especially with vomiting or fever, needs prompt medical assessment and usually a CT scan to confirm the stone.

faqs.question: How long does it take to pass a kidney stone?
faqs.answer: Most small stones pass within one to four weeks once symptoms begin. Stones smaller than 4 mm pass spontaneously about 80% of the time, often within a week or two, while stones of 5-7 mm pass less reliably and may take longer. Stones larger than about 7-10 mm usually need a procedure. If a stone has not passed in four to six weeks, it should be reassessed.

faqs.question: How painful is passing a kidney stone?
faqs.answer: Renal colic from a kidney stone is widely described as one of the most severe pains a person can experience, often compared to or exceeding childbirth. It comes in intense waves as the stone moves and the ureter spasms. The good news is that effective pain relief, usually a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory, controls it well in most people, and the pain stops once the stone passes or is removed.

faqs.question: What causes kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Kidney stones form when urine becomes too concentrated with stone-forming minerals, most often calcium and oxalate, and lacks enough natural inhibitors such as citrate. The leading driver is simply not drinking enough fluid. A diet high in salt and animal protein, obesity, hot climates, certain medical conditions like gout and hyperparathyroidism, and family history all add to the risk.

faqs.question: Can kidney stones go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Small kidney stones, particularly those under 4-5 mm, frequently pass on their own with plenty of fluids and pain control, and no procedure is needed. Larger stones, stones causing uncontrolled pain, or any stone with signs of infection or blocked urine flow need active treatment. A stone that does not pass within several weeks should be reviewed, as a long-impacted stone can damage the kidney.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to get rid of a kidney stone?
faqs.answer: For a stone that needs removal rather than a trial of natural passage, ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy is the fastest route to being stone-free, clearing more than 90% of ureteric stones in a single procedure. Shock wave lithotripsy is non-invasive but less reliable. For a small stone, the fastest natural help is high fluid intake plus an alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin if prescribed.

faqs.question: Will kidney stones come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is common. Without preventive measures, roughly half of people who form a kidney stone develop another within 5 to 10 years. The good news is that recurrence is largely preventable: a structured plan based on the stone's composition and a 24-hour urine analysis — typically more fluids, dietary changes, and sometimes medication — can cut the recurrence rate substantially, often by half or more.

faqs.question: How much water should I drink to prevent kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Aim to drink enough fluid, mostly water, to produce more than 2 to 2.5 litres of urine a day — in practice usually around 2.5 to 3 litres of intake, more in hot weather or with heavy exercise. A simple gauge is urine colour: pale, straw-coloured urine indicates good hydration, while dark urine means you need to drink more. This single measure roughly halves recurrence.

faqs.question: Should I avoid calcium if I get kidney stones?
faqs.answer: No. Cutting calcium out actually increases the risk of the most common, calcium oxalate, stones. Normal dietary calcium taken with meals binds oxalate in the gut so less is absorbed and excreted in urine. The advice is to keep calcium intake normal from food sources such as dairy. It is calcium supplements taken away from food, and high salt, that need more caution.

faqs.question: What foods cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: No single food causes stones, but several raise risk when eaten in excess: high-salt and heavily processed foods increase urinary calcium, large amounts of animal protein lower protective citrate, and concentrated high-oxalate foods such as spinach, rhubarb, beets, and nuts feed calcium oxalate stones. Sugar-sweetened and fructose-rich drinks also raise risk. Moderation, not total avoidance, is the goal for most people.

faqs.question: When should a kidney stone be treated as an emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if you have a fever with flank pain, which suggests an infected blocked kidney, if pain or vomiting cannot be controlled with medication, or if your urine output drops sharply — especially serious if you have only one kidney. Signs of sepsis such as confusion, a racing heart, or feeling profoundly unwell also demand immediate care. These situations need urgent drainage.

faqs.question: Is blood in the urine with a kidney stone dangerous?
faqs.answer: Blood in the urine is a very common feature of a kidney stone, caused by the stone scraping the lining of the urinary tract, and it usually settles once the stone passes or is treated. On its own it is not dangerous. However, blood in the urine that persists after the stone is gone should always be investigated, because it can occasionally point to another cause such as a tumour.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a kidney stone and a UTI?
faqs.answer: A urinary tract infection is a bacterial infection causing burning urination, frequency, urgency, and sometimes fever, with infected urine on testing. A kidney stone is a mineral deposit causing severe wavelike flank pain that radiates to the groin, usually with blood in the urine. They can occur together — a stone blocking an infected system is a medical emergency — but they are different problems.

faqs.question: Can stress or diet alone cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Stress does not directly cause kidney stones, though a hectic routine that leads you to drink too little can contribute indirectly. Diet plays a real but partial role: low fluid intake, high salt, excess animal protein, and sugary drinks all raise risk. Stones usually result from a combination of dehydration, diet, body weight, climate, genetics, and sometimes an underlying medical condition.

faqs.question: What does a kidney stone procedure involve?
faqs.answer: It depends on the stone. Ureteroscopy passes a thin scope up through the bladder to laser the stone, usually as day surgery under anaesthesia, often leaving a temporary stent. Shock wave lithotripsy breaks the stone from outside the body with no incision. Large stones may need percutaneous surgery through a small flank cut with a short hospital stay. Your urologist matches the method to the stone's size and location.

faqs.question: Are kidney stones hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a genuine genetic component. Having a first-degree relative with kidney stones roughly doubles your own risk, partly through inherited differences in how the kidneys handle calcium and partly through shared diet and environment. A few stone disorders, such as cystinuria, are directly inherited and cause recurrent stones from childhood. A family history is worth mentioning to your doctor so prevention can start early.

faqs.question: Can children get kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Yes, and kidney stones in children have become more common over recent decades. In children a stone is more likely to reflect an underlying metabolic or genetic cause, such as cystinuria or a problem with urine chemistry, so children with a stone are usually investigated more thoroughly than adults. The mainstays of prevention — good hydration and a lower-salt diet — apply to children as well.

faqs.question: Does coffee or tea cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: For most people, coffee and tea do not cause kidney stones and may even be associated with a slightly lower risk, since they add to overall fluid intake. The caveat is that tea contains oxalate, so people who form calcium oxalate stones and drink very large amounts of strong tea may be advised to moderate it. Overall, staying well hydrated matters far more than the specific drink.

faqs.question: How are kidney stones diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines the typical history of severe one-sided flank pain with blood in the urine, a urine test, blood tests for kidney function and calcium, and imaging. A non-contrast CT scan is the gold standard, detecting over 95% of stones and showing their exact size and location. Ultrasound is used first in pregnancy. Any stone you pass should be sent for composition analysis to guide prevention.

faqs.question: Can a kidney stone damage my kidney permanently?
faqs.answer: A stone that passes promptly rarely causes lasting harm. Permanent damage happens when a stone blocks urine flow for a long period, when obstruction affects both kidneys or a single kidney, or when an infected blocked kidney is not drained quickly. Large untreated staghorn stones carry a real long-term risk to the kidney. Prompt treatment of obstruction and infection is what protects kidney function.

regionalNames.name: Water infection confusion — distinct from stones

metaTitle: Kidney Stones: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Kidney stones cause sudden severe flank pain and blood in urine. Learn symptoms, how stones pass, treatment options and prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
